ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kebab class (FOODON_03302051); no specific seekh kebab entry exists. Closest parent match used as the kebab class is the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat products — minced/ground meat preparations, grilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for seekh kebab. Closest defensible parent code for grilled minced meat product used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for seekh kebab as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Seekh Kebab is approximately 150g, equivalent to 2–3 skewers, commonly served as a starter or snack alongside mint chutney and onion rings.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Seekh Kebab (Chicken/Mutton) adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, using grilled minced meat composition data. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled ground chicken/lamb (USDA NDB 05064 / 17157) as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. All other values sourced from ICMR-NIN.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein grilled meat preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seekh kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on grilled minced meat preparations with negligible carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g = 3.5 per 100g, predominantly from spice binders). Glycemic load per 150g serving = (15 × 3.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.79, rounded to 0.8. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes as documented in Atkinson et al. (2021).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100g; ~675 mg per 150g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single 150g serving contributes approximately 29% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~15) and high protein content (18.5g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seekh kebab is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and high protein content, which support postprandial blood glucose stability and satiety; however, fat content should be monitored if using fatty mutton cuts.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (14g/100g) with estimated saturated fat (~4.2g/100g) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer chicken over mutton to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, and should limit portion size to manage overall fat load; grilling over charcoal without added oil is the preferred preparation method.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Protein source and inflammatory potential of red meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken seekh kebab is preferred over mutton for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, as red meat may exacerbate inflammation in some patients; the high protein content supports muscle maintenance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seekh Kebab (Chicken/Mutton) is a classic North Indian and Mughlai grilled appetizer made from finely minced chicken or mutton blended with aromatic spices including ginger, garlic, green chilies, coriander, and garam masala. The spiced meat mixture is hand-moulded onto long metal skewers (seekh) and grilled over charcoal in a tandoor or on an open barbecue, producing a smoky, charred exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The dish is typically served with mint chutney and fresh onion rings as a starter, snack, or high-protein side. Nutritionally, seekh kebab is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with approximately 18.5g protein and 14g fat per 100g, and only 3.5g carbohydrate, giving it a very low glycemic index of approximately 15. It provides meaningful amounts of iron (2.2 mg), zinc (3.5 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), niacin (5.5 mg), and selenium (22.5 mcg) per 100g. Sodium content is relatively high at 450 mg per 100g due to seasoning, making it a moderate concern for individuals with hypertension. It is suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns, and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions. The dish is halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified meat and is gluten-free in its traditional form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seekh kebab?
Is seekh kebab good for diabetes?
Is seekh kebab high in protein?
Can I eat seekh kebab on a low-carb diet?
What is the difference between chicken and mutton seekh kebab?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for grilled ground chicken/lamb as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate based on grilled high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. No exact seekh kebab entry exists in any single approved source, hence medium confidence.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy grilled ground chicken (NDB 05064) and ground lamb (NDB 17157) as ICMR-NIN does not report disaggregated fatty acid fractions for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for seekh kebab are not available in ICMR-NIN or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for seekh kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries (all 150g, all is_default=true); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 150g. 5. The input listed ICMR-NIN as the source, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. 6. Vitamin A reported as 5.0 mcg RAE per 100g; this is a low but plausible value for a spiced meat dish with minimal vegetable content. 7. Halal suitability is conditional on use of halal-certified meat. 8. Ontology match types corrected from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the scoring model.
